channel.title: CSR Events - The Council on Strategic Risks
channel.description: Anticipating, Analyzing, and Addressing Systemic Risks

image.title: CSR Events - The Council on Strategic Risks

channel.itunes:author: The Council on Strategic Risks

item.title: Event: Stability Multipliers: Food and Climate Security in a Fractured World
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.category: The Center for Climate and Security
item.description:
Join the Center for Climate & Security (CCS) for a co-hosted, high-level panel discussion exploring the intersections of food, climate change, and international security.

The post Event: Stability Multipliers: Food and Climate Security in a Fractured World first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
The Center for Climate and Security, an institute of the Council on Strategic Risks, the Strategic Climate Risks Initiative, the Ditchley Foundation, and the Center on Global Energy Policy at Columbia University will host a high-level, public panel discussion exploring the intersections of food, climate change, and international security amid a shifting geopolitical order.

Taking place during London Climate Action Week​, this session will convene a panel of experts and policymakers to situate the climate threat within today’s broader geopolitical context, exploring how cascading Earth system changes interact with food systems and the fracturing of international cooperation. The discussion will examine what meaningful climate resilience looks like for governments and institutions navigating both the physical risks and the shifting landscape of global alliances.

​This event will also include a reception offering refreshments and canapés.

Event Information

Stability Multipliers: Food and Climate Security in a Fractured World

Monday, June 22, 2026
18:00 – 20:30 pm BST

Registration required

Register here

Moderator: Erin Sikorsky, Director, Center for Climate and Security, an institute of the Council on Strategic Risks

Speakers to be announced

The post Event: Stability Multipliers: Food and Climate Security in a Fractured World first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Report Launch: Food System Resilience as a Strategic Imperative
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.description:
On Wednesday, June 10, 2026, join authors Tom Ellison, Noah Fritzhand, Alexandra Naegele, and Monica Caparas for a lively discussion on the report's findings and what it can mean for policy going forward.

The post Report Launch: Food System Resilience as a Strategic Imperative first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
In a new joint “Feeding Resilience” report, The Center for Climate and Security, along with the Woodwell Climate Research Center, finds that climate change is causing a two-fold to six-fold increase in the risk of breadbasket failures across Europe and India, which can pose compounding threats to European political cohesion, NATO alliance posture, and global stability, at a moment of cascading geopolitical shocks. This rising risk comes as the world is already facing severe food shocks due to the wars in Iran and Ukraine, and is entering into a potentially unprecedented El Niño season.

“Global Breadbaskets: Food System Resilience as a Strategic Imperative” draws on a range of global crop models to assess the growing risk of climate-driven agricultural failures in Europe and India, ”key producers of wheat, maize, and rice,” and examines the cascading geopolitical consequences of a world in which multiple breadbaskets failures are more likely. Changes in precipitation and heat around the world will not only threaten crops, but also the laborers and infrastructure that translate them into food security. This could lead to shocks rivaling the 2022-2023 food crisis and degrade unity across the European continent and in import-dependent partner countries. Europe should address these risks at multiple levels. Ongoing NATO and EU resilience initiatives should strengthen work on food systems. National, EU, and NATO units should systematize foresight and assessment of food-related instability and hybrid threats. National governments and the EU should preserve investments in global resilience, avoid counterproductive trade restrictions, and invest in agricultural R&D.

On Wednesday, June 10, 2026, join authors Tom Ellison, Noah Fritzhand, Alexandra Naegele, and Monica Caparas for a lively discussion on the report’s findings and what it can mean for policy going forward. Special guest Koen Dekeyser, Policy Analyst at ECDPM, will join to provide opening remarks.

Event Information

Global Breadbaskets: Food System Resilience as a Strategic Imperative

Wednesday, June 10, 2026
10:00–11:00am ET
Online-only

Register via Zoom

The post Report Launch: Food System Resilience as a Strategic Imperative first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Webinar: AI in Professional Military Education: Friend, Foe, or Frenemy?
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.description:
On June 4, the Converging Risks Lab will convene a virtual Oxford Union–style debate on the merits of regulating AI tools in Professional Military Education (PME). Four speakers will make their case, the audience will vote before and after, and someone's opinion will change. (We hope.)

The post Webinar: AI in Professional Military Education: Friend, Foe, or Frenemy? first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
The Converging Risks Lab‘s AI and Cognition series rolls on with Event Two — and this time, we’re bringing the debate format. Grab your popcorn (and insights from the opening panel). 

The US military is integrating AI into professional education at speed. This also raises a question worth arguing about in public: if officers are offloading their thinking to a chatbot, are we developing strategic thinkers or very efficient copy-pasters? Should we rely on the good judgment of those in senior service colleges for how best to use these LLMs, or do we do them a disservice by not providing guardrails and guidelines? 

On June 4, the Converging Risks Lab (CRL), an institute of the Council on Strategic Risks,  will convene a virtual Oxford Union–style debate on the merits of regulating AI tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude in Professional Military Education (PME). Four speakers will make their case, the audience will vote before and after, and someone’s opinion will change. (We hope.)

Event Information

The AI Debates: Opening Panel

AI, Cognition, and PME

Thursday, June 4, 2026
12:00 to 1:00 pm ET

Zoom webinar; Registration requested

Register here

For the Proposition: Dr. Melina Uncapher and Dr. Derek Reveron.

For the Opposition: Dr. Wendy Walsh and Col. Kelly “Curly” Ihme

The post Webinar: AI in Professional Military Education: Friend, Foe, or Frenemy? first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: What Happens to Human Thinking When AI Does the Thinking for Us?
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.description:
The purpose of this opening debate was to review what the science says about AI’s effects on the brain—and the areas where the jury is still out.

The post What Happens to Human Thinking When AI Does the Thinking for Us? first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
On April 1st, 2026, the Converging Risks Lab launched a year-long debate series on AI and cognition with a question that’s more urgent than it might first appear.

Last week, the Converging Risks Lab (CRL) of the Council on Strategic Risks convened the opening panel of a year-long series, the AI Debates, examining the impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on human cognition and the implications for US national security. Moderated by CRL Director Caroline Baxter, the conversation brought together three experts with different vantage points: a Microsoft researcher and university lecturer studying AI in knowledge work, an educational technologist focused on how children learn, and a communication scholar working at the locus of human-technology interaction.

The purpose of the conversation was to review what the science says about AI’s effects on the brain and the areas where the jury is still out. This included concerns that AI may be leading to a narrower range of ideas among adults and may be foreclosing critical thinking skills in children, as well as reflections on how we can use new educational approaches to address these risks. The conversation presented a nuanced look at what we might be trading away as we give AI more of our cognitive labor, and offered thoughts for how we may correct for that.

The research is clear, but it’s specific

Dr. Advait Sarkar, an affiliated lecturer at Cambridge University and researcher at Microsoft, opened with a grounding in what the evidence actually shows. Studies, including his own, find that people working with AI tend to produce a narrower range of ideas, report putting in less analytical effort, and remember less of the work they’ve done. That last finding has particular implications for accountability: if you can’t recall how a decision was reached, it’s harder to defend it.

Sarkar was careful not to overclaim: he made it clear that we don’t yet have strong evidence that AI use causes broad cognitive decline across the board. What we do have, however, is evidence that one’s memory of and critical engagement with a specific task tends to diminish when handed over to AI to manage. Sarkar also pointed to early hints of something of greater concern: “intuition rust.” A year-long study of oncologists found that while AI raised their diagnostic accuracy, their subjective confidence in their own unaided judgment declined over time. The good news, Sarkar argued, is that the aviation and nuclear industries solved analogous problems through deliberate training and better interface design. 

The deeper risk may be foreclosure, not atrophy

Timothy Cook, founder of ConnectedClassroom.org, shifted the focus to the impact of this technology on developing minds and the associated long-term cost. The conversation about cognitive atrophy, Cook argued, implicitly assumes people already have the skills they’re at risk of losing. While this is largely true for adults with fully developed brains, this is not the case for children. The question, therefore, isn’t only whether AI erodes critical thinking but whether it impedes critical thinking from developing in the first place.

Cook argued that prior technologies (e.g., calculators, GPS, Google) offloaded nouns – storage, retrieval, operation – but a human still had to supply the reasoning. AI, however, offloads verbs: synthesizing, evaluating, judging. When that substitution happens during the developmental period of the human prefrontal cortex, from birth until the mid-20s, the consequences could be a lasting cognitive deficit. Cook also raised the risk of the homogenization of ideas and thoughts that happens when millions of people route their thinking through the same large language model. The consequence is that cognitive diversity narrows, and with it, resilience to change.

History offers a map, but not a guarantee

Dr. Sorin Adam Matei, a professor at Purdue’s Brian Lamb School of Communication, brought a historical lens. Societies have abandoned skills before—most of us can’t plow a field or make a wooden spoon—and survived, because structured education evolved to fill the gap. The Industrial Revolution’s disruption to agrarian skills was eventually absorbed. The question now is whether we can engineer an equivalent educational response before the window closes.

In the near term, he argued, the priority is guardrails: clear institutional protocols for AI use in mission-critical environments, for example, within the US Department of Defense, that define which tasks AI is appropriate for and who bears responsibility for outcomes. In the longer term, foundational intellectual skills—logic, argumentation, systems thinking—need to become mandatory in the curriculum before students ever encounter AI as a substitute for those capabilities.

So what?

Having scoped the factual landscape, the ensuing three AI Debates will apply these considerations and conclusions to three areas of US national security: the education of military officers, intelligence and its tradecraft, and the conduct of warfare itself. The first of these debates, on professional military education (PME), is scheduled for June 5th.

Click here to to pre-register for the June 5, 2026 debate. More information to come soon.

To dig deeper:

Cook, T. (n.d.). The Algorithmic Mind [Column]. Psychology Today

Sourati, Z., Ziabari, A. S., & Dehghani, M. (2026). The homogenizing effect of large language models on human expression and thought

Gerlich, Michael (2025). AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking

Lee, H. et al. (2025). The Impact of Generative AI on Critical Thinking: Self-Reported Reductions in Cognitive Effort and Confidence Effects From a Survey of Knowledge Workers

Sarkar, A. (2024). AI Should Challenge, Not Obey

Tankelevitch, L. et al. (2024). The Metacognitive Demands and Opportunities of Generative AI

Matei, S., Graves, K., & Benson D. (2023). What Should a Strategist Know and Do, and WhyMatei, S. & Reed, K. (2025). Mission Command’s Asymmetric Advantage Through AI-Driven Data Management

The post What Happens to Human Thinking When AI Does the Thinking for Us? first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Event Summary: The Opportunities and Challenges of Cloud Laboratories in Producing AI-Ready Biological Data
item.dc:creator: Crystal Grant
item.description:
In coordination with the BIOTech Caucus, CSR and the Engineering Biology Research Consortium (EBRC) co-hosted a bipartisan briefing on Capitol Hill exploring how Congress can strengthen US leadership at the intersection of biotechnology and AI.

The post Event Summary: The Opportunities and Challenges of Cloud Laboratories in Producing AI-Ready Biological Data first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
Rapid advances at the intersection of biotechnology, artificial intelligence, and automation are opening a new frontier for US innovation and security. Cloud laboratories—digitally connected, automated facilities that execute biological experiments remotely and often in conjunction with AI Agents—are expanding research capacity, accelerating discovery, and strengthening the infrastructure that underpins the American bioeconomy. As shown by recent legislative actions in the House and Senate, and the executive branch’s Programmable Cloud Laboratory Testbed and Genesis Mission, the US government is advancing automation to produce high-quality, AI-ready data. 

On March 3rd, The Council on Strategic Risks (CSR) and the Engineering Biology Research Consortium (EBRC), in coordination with the BIOTech Caucus, co-hosted a Bipartisan Briefing on Capitol Hill exploring how Congress can strengthen US leadership in this rapidly evolving space. The discussion highlighted both the opportunities and the governance challenges arising from the continued integration of AI and automation into biotechnology research and manufacturing. The panelists included:

  • Edward You, Former FBI Supervisory Special Agent and Founder & Principal Consultant, EHY Consulting LLC 
  • D.J. Kleinbaum, Ph.D, Co-founder of Emerald Cloud Lab (ECL)
  • Whitney Bowman-Zatzkin, Co-founder/Director of the Bioeconomy Information Sharing and Analysis Center (BIO-ISAC) 
  • Steven Moss, Ph.D, Senior Policy Advisor, National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology (NSCEB)

Data is a strategic asset

“AI needs data more than data needs AI.”

Speakers emphasized that biological data is becoming a strategic asset, and described investment in cloud labs as a way to create enough data to power future biological AI models. Drawing a parallel between the US government investing in cloud labs and the government of Taiwan’s early investments in TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company), a panelist stated that the US government could invest in cloud labs as a resource, allowing researchers to capitalize on that investment by sharing in the use of these scientific labs. 

Cloud labs enable scientists to design experiments to be conducted remotely through software interfaces, and then receive the results in the form of standardized experimental data. Panelists described a network of cloud labs as an excellent opportunity both for reducing variability in experimentation and in collecting metadata, such as data on how the experimental data is being generated (e.g., physical conditions of the room, specifications of the lab equipment used). 

Investment in cloud labs could bolster competitiveness in biotech

“We’re in the midst of a global biotech race. Space races are won by out-innovating.”

Panelists compared the biotech landscape to a space race, insisting that playing defense won’t be sufficient given that US competitors, like China, are increasingly prioritizing investment in biotechnologies, especially in convergence with AI. Panelists addressed that, outside of pandemic preparedness, falling behind in biotech could potentially be exploited across a number of domains and application spaces, such as agriculture. 

One common perspective was that cloud laboratories could strengthen US competitiveness by increasing experimental throughput, accelerating research timelines, and enabling the generation of large-scale biological datasets. These capabilities could provide the United States with greater resilience and agility in responding to emerging technological and security challenges.

Cyberbiosecurity is vital to national security

“Biotech is not [currently] part of national security and is not deemed critical infrastructure.”

Several panelists described cybersecurity concerns around the generation of biological data via automated laboratories. Automated laboratories rely on networked equipment, software platforms, and remote access to conduct experiments, which are useful capabilities that also introduce new vulnerabilities and provide additional areas that could be subject to cyberattacks. 

Moreover, to strengthen the bioeconomy, panelists emphasized the need for biotechnology to be recognized as strategically relevant to national security and to be designated as critical infrastructure to be protected. Formal designation by the US Government can affect the availability of federal cybersecurity resources, information-sharing mechanisms, and coordinated protections.

Citing the example of cyberattacks on companies involved in COVID-19 vaccine distribution and the attacks’ attempted negative effects on bioresilience, cyberbiosecurity of cloud labs must also be considered as they become more widely integrated in US biotech infrastructure. 

Current legislative initiatives can shape cloud labs in the United States

“We need to build and build quickly.”

Congress has begun examining how federal policy can support emerging experimental infrastructure such as cloud laboratories, with some legislation stemming from recommendations made in NSCEB’s 2025 report. Two bipartisan bills relevant to cloud labs have been introduced in the Senate. The bill (S.2676) introduced by Senators Young (R-IN) and Kim (D-NJ) calls for building more cloud labs, while the bill (S.3468) introduced by Senators Fetterman (D-PA) and Budd (R-NC) emphasizes the importance of interoperability. Panelists stressed that both approaches are necessary. Expanding infrastructure can increase access to automated experimentation, while system interoperability ensures that data generated across different cloud labs can be shared, integrated, and reused effectively. The day after the panel, a counterpart bill to the Young/Kim legislation was introduced in the House (H.R.7801). Together, these bipartisan proposals signal that Congress is increasingly interested in modernizing research infrastructure to support the convergence of biology, artificial intelligence, and robotics.

Conclusion

Central to the entire discussion was the unanimous belief that automated laboratories represent a key way for the US to collect the immense volume of high-quality, AI-ready data needed to stay competitive and secure. At the same time, participants emphasized the need to ensure this data remains well protected. They shared their belief that now is the moment for the United States to move biotech forward, noting the benefits of current legislative efforts around cloud labs. These messages were well received across the bipartisan audience of congressional staffers, tech industry representatives, and national security specialists.

The post Event Summary: The Opportunities and Challenges of Cloud Laboratories in Producing AI-Ready Biological Data first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Watch: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.category: The Center for Climate and Security
item.description:
Watch the recent webinar where CCS experts discuss the energy, water, and food implications of the ongoing war in Iran.

The post Watch: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
In case you missed it, watch the recent webinar where experts from the Center for Climate and Security discuss the energy, water, and food implications of the ongoing war in Iran. The discussion was moderated by CCS Director Erin Sikorsky and featured speakers Tom Ellison, CCS Deputy Director, Swathi Veeravalli, CCS Advisory Board Member, and CCS Non-resident Fellows Dr. Cullen Hendrix, Peter Schwartzstein, and Dr. Marcus D. King.

The post Watch: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Event: Did an LLM Write This? And other questions from the convergence of AI and national security
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.description:
Over the course of 2026, CRL will hold a series of Oxford Union-style debates with experts in the AI, national security, intelligence, and military domains. Each debate will revolve around a motion and put two teams in the Proposition and Opposition camps to explore the contours of the topic.

The post Event: Did an LLM Write This? And other questions from the convergence of AI and national security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
As the world witnesses the war in Iran and the unfolding regional conflict, it is also witnessing the tangible results of decades of work on Artificial Intelligence (AI), including the collaboration between major AI companies and the US Department of Defense. A technology that was first imagined 70 years ago at a summer conference in New Hampshire has now been comprehensively diffused throughout our economy and society – from the classroom to the bathroom to the Situation Room to the war in Iran itself, where it is being deployed in “Operation Epic Fury.” The US military’s Maven Smart System is currently using AI to turn intelligence into prioritized lists of targets faster than humans can. 

As AI has spread rapidly throughout facets of the average American’s daily life, certain terms have started cropping up relative to the use of one aspect of AI, large language models (LLMs), that hint at some of the consequences of this technology – disempowerment, cognitive offloading, and cognitive privacy. Worryingly, some of the most noteworthy leaders in the AI field have left it behind, citing fears of “a world in peril.” 

It all begs the question: Are we appropriately reckoning with the potential consequences of AI as we use it? 

To begin grappling with this tectonic technological evolution, the Council on Strategic Risks is bringing the conversation down to the granular level to interrogate the implications of widespread AI adoption for one of the most complex pieces of technology ever created: the human brain. What does AI, and specifically LLMs, mean for human cognition and critical thinking, and what implications does that have for the core functions and capabilities of the national security workforce?

Event Information

The AI Debates: Opening Panel

Did an LLM Write This? And other questions from the convergence of AI and national security

Wednesday, April 1, 2026
12:00 to 1:00 pm ET

Zoom webinar; Registration requested

Register here

The Converging Risks Lab is taking this question head-on with a four-part series centered around one specific consequence: the impact of AI on human cognition. The implications of these consequences have bearing on the future of the national security workforce, both military and civilian, as well as the decision of whether and how to govern or regulate AI.

To kick off this series, on April 1st, we will tackle the question keeping national security leaders up at night: Does using AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini ruin our minds, or are we just anxious about anything that makes life easier? 

Three experts – Timothy Cook, Dr. Soren Matei, and Dr. Advait Sarkar – will help us figure out whether cognitive offloading is the crisis some say it is, or if AI actually helps. This panel will lay the groundwork for three subsequent Oxford Union-style debates that will apply this question to three facets of the national security world: professional military education, intelligence analysis, and defense contracting. 

Over the course of 2026, CRL will hold a series of Oxford Union-style debates with experts in the AI, national security, intelligence, and military domains. Each debate will revolve around a motion and put two teams in the Proposition and Opposition camps to explore the contours of the topic.   

  • April 1: AI and cognition. This opening panel will set the scene for the three debates. Three panelists, Timothy Cook, Dr. Michael Gerlich, and Dr. Advait Sarkar, will interrogate the science done to date on the topic of genAI impact on user cognition and bound the risk space.
  • June: The first debate: AI and Professional Military Education. This first Oxford Union-style debate will focus on how AI is being used across the military services to educate officers. The debate will explore the extent to which the risks of widespread LLM use in the PME classroom are unique, important, and worthy of further study.
  • September: The second debate: AI and Intelligence. This second debate will explore how AI is being used for intelligence collection and analysis, and reckon with the consequences of increasingly relying on AI to inform or drive decisions made on those products. 
  • December: The third debate: AI and the Military. This final debate will examine how LLMs are being developed to satisfy military requirements and the implications of AI-altered cognition on judgment and critical thinking for uniformed personnel.

The post Event: Did an LLM Write This? And other questions from the convergence of AI and national security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Event: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.category: The Center for Climate and Security
item.description:
Join the Center for Climate & Security (CCS) for a webinar and audience Q&A on the food, water, & energy implications of the US-Israeli conflict with Iran.

The post Event: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
The ongoing US and Israeli conflict with Iran has expanded across the Middle East, and is already upending economic markets and regional security. On March 10, 2026, the Center for Climate & Security will host a webinar and audience Q&A on the food, water, & energy implications of the US-Israeli conflict with Iran.

Event Information

The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security

Tuesday, March 10, 2026
12:00 to 1:00 pm ET

Zoom webinar; Registration requested

Register here

Speakers to include:

Tom Ellison, Deputy Director, Center for Climate and Security

Tom Ellison is Deputy Director of the Center for Climate and Security (CCS). His expertise includes the security implications of food and water crises, migration, climate mis/disinformation, geoengineering, and the energy transition. He is published and quoted in a range of outlets, such as Lawfare, Just Security, PBS NewsHour, and TRT World. 

Prior to joining CCS, Tom spent a decade in the US intelligence community, where he helped expand analysis of the security and foreign policy implications of climate change for senior policymakers across the US government. Prior to that, he received commendations for intelligence analysis of terrorist threats, insurgencies, and political turmoil during the Arab Spring. He traveled extensively in Europe and the Middle East, including war zones.

Tom is an Arabic speaker and holds an M.A. in Security Studies from Georgetown University and a B.A. in Political Science from Colgate University. He also serves on the Board of Homes Not Borders (serving the refugee and forced migrant population in Washington, DC) and writes humor in outlets like McSweeney’s and The New Yorker.

Dr. Cullen Hendrix, Non-resident Fellow, Center for Climate and Security

Dr. Cullen Hendrix (@cullenhendrix) is Senior Fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, and Non-resident Senior Fellow at the Center for Climate & Security. Recently, Dr. Hendrix was a Specially Appointed Research Professor at Hiroshima University in Japan, where he is a researcher with the Network for Education and Research on Peace and Sustainability (NERPS). Prior to joining PIIE full-time, he was Professor of International Studies at the Korbel School, University of Denver.

His 30+ peer-reviewed articles on the relationships between international markets, natural resources, and conflict, as well as the economic and security implications of climate change, have appeared in journals ranging from Nature, Nature Climate Change, Biological Reviews, Ecology and Society, Marine Policy, and Global Environmental Change to the British Journal of Political Science, Journal of Conflict Resolution, Political Geography, and Journal of Peace Research. He is coauthor, with Marcus Noland, of Confronting the Curse: The Economics and Geopolitics of Natural Resource Governance (2014).

Hendrix has authored reports published by or consulted for organizations including the Asian Development Bank, the Chicago Council on Global Affairs, the National Intelligence Council, Oxfam America, USAID, and the World Food Programme, among others. His published commentaries have appeared in Foreign Policy, Barron’s, War on the Rocks, and the Washington Post, and his perspective has informed reporting for the New York Times, Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg, and National Public Radio. He was a contributing author to the 2022 IPCC report, for which he assessed the implications of climate change for threats to peace and human mobility.

His research has been supported by the National Science Foundation’s Coupled Natural and Human Systems program, the US Department of Defense Minerva Initiative, the Carnegie Corporation, and the Smith Richardson Foundation, and he was a pre-doctoral fellow at the Peace Research Institute, Oslo. He holds a PhD and MA from the University of California, San Diego, where he was a fellow of the Institute on Global Conflict and Cooperation, and a BA from Kalamazoo College.

Swathi Veeravalli, Advisory Board Member, Center for Climate and Security

Swathi Veeravalli  is a member of the Center for Climate and Security Advisory Board, and an an interdisciplinary research scientist at the Geospatial Research Laboratory, Engineer Research and Development Center, US Army Corps of Engineers. Since 2010, she has been Principal Investigator on several basic and applied research projects where she focuses on developing the capability to better understand the impact of climate variability upon humans and the environment.

Prior to government service, Swathi was a water analyst at Global Water Intelligence, where she assessed and monitored water, wastewater and desalination projects of the international water industry. She also helped coordinate a World Bank longitudinal study examining the productive uses of piped water in Kenya.  Swathi has several years’ experience in international development, youth engagement and  advocacy.  Swathi holds a Master’s of Science in Water Science, Policy & Management from the University of Oxford. At Oxford, her research focused on peri-urban water development with fieldwork in Kenya and Botswana.  Swathi also holds a BA in International Affairs with concentrations in African Politics and Development from the George Washington University.  She has worked and travelled in over 68 different countries.

Peter Schwartzstein, Non-resident Fellow, Center for Climate and Security

Peter Schwartzstein is an environmental journalist and a non-resident research fellow at the Center for Climate and Security. Based first in Cairo and now Athens, he has reported on water, food security, and conflict-climate issues across more than 30 countries in the Middle East, Africa, and South Asia. Peter mostly writes for National Geographic, but has also contributed frequent articles to Bloomberg, the BBC, and the New York Times, among other publications.

In addition to journalism, Peter regularly consults on climate security and other topics for the likes of UNEP, UNICEF, ICRC, and Amnesty International, and mentors young environmental reporters. Prior to his move to the Middle East, he worked for Reuters in London. Peter holds BA and MA degrees from Trinity College, Dublin, and is a member of the Overseas Press Club, Royal Geographic Society, Society of Environmental Journalists and Frontline Freelance Register. He is also a Global fellow at The Wilson Center and a TED fellow.

Email: pflschwartz (at) gmail.com

Dr. Marcus D. King, Non-resident Fellow, Center for Climate and Security

Marcus D. King is the Vice Chair and Secretary of the Council on Strategic Risks Governing Board. He is also a Non-resident Senior Fellow and member of the Advisory Board at the Center for Climate and Security. He has extensive experience with climate change and energy issues in academia, policy-research organizations and the U.S. government.

Dr. King is a Professor of the Practice in Environment and International Affairs at Georgetown University’s Walsh School of Foreign Service and Earth Commons Institute. Formerly, he was the John O. Rankin Associate Professor of International Affairs and Director of the International Affairs Program at George Washington University’s Elliott School of International Affairs. Dr. King was previously Associate Research Professor and Director of Research where he worked with the Elliott School’s nine centers and institutes to coordinate over $30 million in faculty sponsored research proposals. Prior to these positions Dr. King served as a globalization planning fellow and special assistant for international initiatives in Georgetown University’s Office of the President. He was also appointed as an adjunct assistant professor. King’s teaching and academic research focus on field of environmental security. His current book project examines linkages between water scarcity and violent extremism.

Dr. King joined the Elliott School from CNA Corporation’s Center for Naval Analyses where he led studies for U.S. government agencies on climate change security, resilience, adaptation and energy security. He was also project director for the CNA Military Advisory Board (MAB), an elite group of former admirals and generals that launched landmark reports on how these topics affect U.S. national security. Prior to CNA, Dr. King was Research Director of the Sustainable Energy Institute; and Senior Manager for Energy and Security Programs at a private consultancy.

In the 1990s, Dr. King held appointments in the Office of the Secretary of Defense as a foreign affairs specialist in environmental security where he represented the United States for negotiation of the Kyoto Protocol on climate change and the Office of the Secretary of Energy where he directly supported the Deputy Secretary and participated in negotiations on the peaceful uses of nuclear energy with the Russian Federation.

Dr. King has presented at academic conferences, written peer-reviewed articles and book chapters and delivered invited lectures to several governments and international organizations. He has been cited by The New York Times, Newsweek, Slate, and Vice Magazine among others and appeared on Al Jazeera.

A graduate of the Georgetown University’s School of Foreign Service, King holds a Ph.D. in international relations from The Fletcher School of Law and Diplomacy at Tufts University.

The post Event: The Iran War: Implications for Food, Water, and Energy Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Event Summary: Food on the Frontlines? Evaluating the Future of Defense in an Unstable Climate (Munich Security Conference 2026)
item.dc:creator: Noah Fritzhand
item.description:
CCS and the International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT) lead an official MSC 2026 Side Event as part of CCS’ Feeding Resilience Program to discuss the nexus of climate change, food, and national security in the NATO context.

The post Event Summary: Food on the Frontlines? Evaluating the Future of Defense in an Unstable Climate (Munich Security Conference 2026) first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
Colombia’s Minister of Defense, Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez, talking about climate change at the MSC. (Lukas Barth-Tuttas/MSC)

In one of the opening panels of the 2026 Munich Security Conference (MSC), Colombia’s Minister of Defense, Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez, remarked: “…things that happen in one part of the world can affect the entire world. The threat of climate change is real and we are here, of course, to solve that problem together.”

Referring to the ongoing torrential rains that have devastated Colombia by displacing thousands of families, destroying key infrastructure, disrupting access to food, and decimating crucial crop yields, Minister Suárez’s comments hold true across the world as these threats do not stop at one country’s borders. While many defense leaders recognize the reality of climate threats, the interconnections between climate change, food and agriculture, and national security remain insufficiently incorporated into many countries’ security planning.

At the 2026 MSC, the Center for Climate and Security (CCS) and the International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT) convened defense, climate, and agri-food leaders for an official MSC Side Event as part of CCS’ Feeding Resilience Program to discuss this nexus in the NATO context.

Photo from CCS and CIMMYT official MSC Side Event. (Marcel Kusch / MSC)

As NATO revises its baseline resilience requirements for member states, the Alliance has an opportunity to make sure these requirements institutionalize “stability multipliers” that better prepare for the impacts of extreme weather and climate change on critical infrastructure, food and water security, and energy systems in a fractured geopolitical landscape. As part of the Chatham House Rule discussion, leaders deliberated:

  1. The primary impacts on domestic resilience in European and NATO countries from global food insecurity, extreme weather, and other climate-driven threats; 
  2. Opportunities for resilience investments and policy approaches that provide co-benefits to national defense and geopolitical positioning; 
  3. How peace and security actors can better plan for the food and climate security challenges to come – and vice versa.

Participants noted that global security is deeply intertwined with food systems and climate change as primary factors that shape instability and the operating landscape. Prolonged droughts in agriculturally dependent regions, such as those in Somalia, were highlighted as a key destabilizing climate impact. Emphasis was placed on the need to shift planning, across all sectors, toward a ‘resilient by design’ model.

Several participants from the defense sector explained that militaries are attuned to climate-related risks and vulnerabilities because they are often first responders during disasters, operate in more challenging environments, and work with partner countries that are highly concerned about the immediate impacts of climate change (such as the Pacific Islands). There are, however, still significant gaps in operationalizing climate considerations within military planning, and participants called for the development of strengthened risk assessment tools, deepened collaboration with allies, and increased investments in resilience as a strategic advantage.

Throughout the conversation, participants raised concerns about cuts to development and humanitarian funding amidst an increasingly transactional geopolitical moment. Reductions or eliminations of aid, combined with dramatic increases in defense spending, could generate a vicious cycle in which unmet humanitarian needs exacerbate instability and lead to increased security burdens.

The session concluded with a shared recognition that integrating food, climate, and security considerations is not optional, but essential to long-term global resilience and stability.

Read more on food security:

  • Munich Security Conference Food Security Task Force Joint Statement: Resilience of Food Systems as Forward Defense
  • Erin Sikorsky and Benedetta Berti, Weaponized Food and the New Frontline of Security
  • Stability Multipliers: Food Security, Climate Change, and The Future of NATO Resilience 
  • Feeding Resilience: Recap of CCS and Kansas State Event on Risk and Readiness in Food Security
  • Feeding Resilience: Ethiopia

The post Event Summary: Food on the Frontlines? Evaluating the Future of Defense in an Unstable Climate (Munich Security Conference 2026) first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.

item.title: Briefing: Harnessing AI and Robotics to Strengthen America’s Biotechnology Leadership and Security
item.dc:creator: Council on Strategic Risks
item.description:
On March 3, 2026, CSR and EBRC, in coordination with the BIOTech Caucus, are co-hosting a bipartisan Hill briefing exploring how Congress can strengthen U.S. leadership in this rapidly evolving space.

The post Briefing: Harnessing AI and Robotics to Strengthen America’s Biotechnology Leadership and Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
item.content:encoded:
On March 3, 2026, the Council on Strategic Risks (CSR) and the Engineering Biology Research Consortium (EBRC),  in coordination with the BIOTech Caucus, will co-host a bipartisan briefing on Capitol Hill exploring how the US Congress can strengthen US leadership in this rapidly evolving space.

The discussion will highlight both the opportunities and the governance challenges arising from the continued integration of AI and automation into biotechnology research and manufacturing. By convening experts from across disciplines, it will explore how Congress can advance US competitiveness, safeguard national security, and guide innovation by responsibly harnessing the combined power of AI, robotics, and biology.

Event Information

Harnessing AI and Robotics to Strengthen America’s Biotechnology Leadership and Security

Tuesday, March 3, 2026
9:00 to 10:00 am

The Rayburn House Office Building
Room 2075

In-person only; Registration requested

Register here

Background:

Rapid advances in biotechnology, powered by artificial intelligence and automation, are opening a new frontier for US innovation and security. Cloud laboratories—digitally connected, automated facilities that execute biological experiments remotely and often in conjunction with AI Agents—are expanding research capacity, accelerating discovery, and strengthening the infrastructure that underpins the American bioeconomy. By linking intelligent systems and robotics to biotechnology workflows, cloud labs are redefining what innovation looks like in the life sciences. 

Discussion themes:

  • Opportunities and risks at the convergence of AI, automation, and the biotech ecosystem
  • How the use of AI Agents and automation is affecting the pace of biological research
  • The role cloud labs have in innovation and competition (especially with China)
  • Biosecurity and cybersecurity risks associated with cloud labs

Speakers to include:

Edward You, Founder & Principal Consultant, EHY Consulting LLC 

Edward You is the Founder of EHY Consulting LLC, a boutique advisory firm focused on the national security implications of biotechnology, artificial intelligence, and data-driven innovation. A former FBI Supervisory Special Agent, he spent more than twenty years shaping U.S. government approaches to biosecurity and emerging technologies at the intersection of science, policy, and intelligence.

During his federal career, Ed served in roles supporting the FBI, the White House, and the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, where he contributed to national policy development on biotechnology, AI, and the broader bioeconomy. He represented the United States at the United Nations Biological Weapons Convention and supported international biosecurity and nonproliferation engagement with partners across Asia, Europe, and Latin America.

Ed has worked extensively with research universities, industry leaders, and government agencies to strengthen research integrity, protect sensitive data, and anticipate strategic risks arising from emerging technologies. He is known for translating complex technical issues into clear policy-relevant insights for decision-makers.

Featured on CBS’s 60 Minutes for his leadership in safeguarding biotechnology, Ed emphasizes a core principle that guides his work today: technology’s future depends on trust.

D.J. Kleinbaum, Ph.D, Co-founder of Emerald Cloud Lab (ECL)

D.J. Kleinbaum, Ph.D. is the Co-founder of Emerald Cloud Lab (ECL), the world’s first and largest Cloud Lab. Prior to founding ECL, D.J. co-founded Emerald Therapeutics, which focused on developing a novel antiviral nanotechnology to detect and instruct the apoptosis of infected cells. D.J. holds a Ph.D. in Chemistry from Stanford University, where he studied nucleic acid chemistry under the direction of Professor Eric Kool. He also holds an undergraduate degree in Biological Sciences with a minor in Chemistry and concentrations in Computational Biology and Biochemistry from Carnegie Mellon University. He serves on the Advisory Board for the Carnegie Mellon University’s Swartz Center for Entrepreneurship, the Technical Consortium on International Standards for DNA Synthesis Screening, and advises start-up companies.

Whitney Bowman-Zatzkin, Co-founder/Director of the Bioeconomy Information Sharing and Analysis Center (BIO-ISAC) 

Whitney Bowman-Zatzkin, MPA, MSR, is the co-founder/director of the Bioeconomy Information Sharing and Analysis Center, BIO-ISAC (isac.bio), a global nonprofit fortifying cybersecurity resilience for biology-enabled systems across the bioeconomy—everything from dirt to data. She brings two decades of leadership to connecting dots that ignite transformative change for the greater good. 

Her past work includes the Data Use Transparency and Impact Report for Invitae (Labcorp); Digital Therapeutics Alliance, Project Baseline, Access our Medicine – a project of Mindset Foundation, and Script your Future, a medication adherence project commended by the U.S. Surgeon General and Members of Congress. A multi-project grantee of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation, she was Managing Director of Flip the Clinic; Co-PI for Scouting Health, an horizon-hunting effort with Westat; and led the Great Challenges at TEDMED, hosting 50+ of the toughest conversations in health care. 

Whitney is currently completing her PhD at Virginia Tech and has Masters degrees from the University of Connecticut. Her publications include cyberbiosecurity, defense strategies for the bioeconomy, data use transparency, and the role of pharmacists in patient care. 

A sought-after systems change architect and leading expert on cyberbiosecurity, she champions global efforts to protect the world’s investments in biotechnology. 

Steven Moss, Senior Policy Advisor, National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology (NSCEB)

Steven Moss is a Senior Policy Advisor for the National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology (NSCEB) where he focuses on policy around the convergence of emerging areas of science and technology with biotechnology. Prior to joining the NSCEB, Steven was a Senior Program Officer at the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM). At NASEM he worked on their Board on Life Sciences, focused on a portfolio of projects related to advancing the bioeconomy and the future of biotechnology. Steven comes from a laboratory background, having spent 10 years in chemistry and biology laboratory settings. Much of his lab work focused on understanding the molecular mechanisms of disease with projects on infectious bacteria, cancer, and chronic pain. Steven also serves as the Chair of the American Chemical Society’s Committee on Environment and Sustainability and a judge for the international Genetically Engineering Machine (iGEM) competition. Steven holds a Ph.D. in Chemistry and Chemical Biology from the University of California, San Francisco, and a B.S. in Biochemistry from American University.

The post Briefing: Harnessing AI and Robotics to Strengthen America’s Biotechnology Leadership and Security first appeared on The Council on Strategic Risks.
